title: San Antonio, Texas

infobox.value: [Bexar](/wiki/Bexar_County,_Texas "Bexar County, Texas"), [Comal](/wiki/Comal_County,_Texas "Comal County, Texas"), [Medina](/wiki/Medina_County,_Texas "Medina County, Texas")
[Saint Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Saint_Anthony_of_Padua "Saint Anthony of Padua")
[Gina Ortiz Jones](/wiki/Gina_Ortiz_Jones "Gina Ortiz Jones") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"))
[San Antonio City Council](/wiki/San_Antonio_City_Council "San Antonio City Council")

* [18th](/wiki/List_of_North_American_cities_by_population "List of North American cities by population") in North America * [7th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States * [2nd](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Texas "List of municipalities in Texas") in Texas

([/ˌsæn ænˈtoʊnioʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAN an-TOH-nee-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Spanish for "[Saint Anthony](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua")") is a city in the U.S. state of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"). It is the [seventh-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States, the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Texas "List of municipalities in Texas") in Texas (after [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston")), and the second-most populous city in the [Southern U.S.](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), with a population of 1.43 million at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"). The [San Antonio metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_San_Antonio "Greater San Antonio"), with an estimated 2.76 million residents, ranks as the [third-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_Texas_metropolitan_areas "List of Texas metropolitan areas") in Texas and the [24th-largest](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the nation.[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13)[[7]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7) It is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Bexar County](/wiki/Bexar_County,_Texas "Bexar County, Texas").

**San Antonio** ([/ˌsæn ænˈtoʊnioʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAN an-TOH-nee-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Spanish for "[Saint Anthony](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua")") is a city in the U.S. state of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"). It is the [seventh-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States, the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Texas "List of municipalities in Texas") in Texas (after [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston")), and the second-most populous city in the [Southern U.S.](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), with a population of 1.43 million at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"). The [San Antonio metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_San_Antonio "Greater San Antonio"), with an estimated 2.76 million residents, ranks as the [third-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_Texas_metropolitan_areas "List of Texas metropolitan areas") in Texas and the [24th-largest](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the nation.[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13)[[7]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7) It is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Bexar County](/wiki/Bexar_County,_Texas "Bexar County, Texas").

The area was named "San Antonio" in 1691 by a Spanish expedition in honor of Saint [Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua"). A [Spanish mission and colonial outpost](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas") was founded there in 1718, and San Antonio became the first chartered civil settlement in present-day Texas in 1731. It was part of the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), then the [Mexican Republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic") from 1821 to 1836, before joining the United States after the annexation of the Republic of Texas in 1846.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Straddling the regional divide between [South](/wiki/South_Texas "South Texas") and [Central Texas](/wiki/Central_Texas "Central Texas"), San Antonio anchors the southwestern corner of an urban [megaregion](/wiki/Megaregion "Megaregion") colloquially known as the [Texas Triangle](/wiki/Texas_Triangle "Texas Triangle"). It lies about 80 miles (130 km) from [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") along the [I-35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Texas "Interstate 35 in Texas") corridor, and together, the [San Antonio–Austin metroplex](/wiki/San_Antonio%E2%80%93Austin_metroplex "San Antonio–Austin metroplex") is home to approximately 5 million people.[[17]](#cite_note-sa-atxmetro-17)

San Antonio is home to five 18th-century Spanish frontier missions, including [The Alamo](/wiki/Alamo_Mission "Alamo Mission") and those preserved in [San Antonio Missions National Historical Park](/wiki/San_Antonio_Missions_National_Historical_Park "San Antonio Missions National Historical Park"), which were collectively designated as [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 2015.[[18]](#cite_note-PBS-SanAn-18) Major attractions include the [River Walk](/wiki/San_Antonio_River_Walk "San Antonio River Walk"), [Tower of the Americas](/wiki/Tower_of_the_Americas "Tower of the Americas"), [SeaWorld San Antonio](/wiki/SeaWorld_San_Antonio "SeaWorld San Antonio"), [Six Flags Fiesta Texas](/wiki/Six_Flags_Fiesta_Texas "Six Flags Fiesta Texas"), and the [Alamo Bowl](/wiki/Alamo_Bowl "Alamo Bowl"). The city also hosts the [San Antonio Stock Show & Rodeo](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stock_Show_%26_Rodeo "San Antonio Stock Show & Rodeo") and is home to the five-time [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") champion [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs"). San Antonio welcomes approximately 32 million visitors annually and is a key military hub, hosting several major [U.S. Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") facilities, including [Fort Sam Houston](/wiki/Fort_Sam_Houston "Fort Sam Houston") and nearby bases such as [Lackland](/wiki/Lackland_Air_Force_Base "Lackland Air Force Base") and [Randolph Air Force Bases](/wiki/Randolph_Air_Force_Base "Randolph Air Force Base"). The city is also home to four [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies, the [South Texas Medical Center](/wiki/South_Texas_Medical_Center "South Texas Medical Center"), and the [largest majority-Hispanic population](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_large_Hispanic_populations "List of U.S. cities with large Hispanic populations") in the U.S., with 64% of residents identifying as Hispanic.[[19]](#cite_note-census-19)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of San Antonio](/wiki/History_of_San_Antonio "History of San Antonio")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of San Antonio](/wiki/Timeline_of_San_Antonio "Timeline of San Antonio").

At the time of European encounter, the [Payaya people](/wiki/Payaya_people "Payaya people") lived near the [San Antonio River Valley](/wiki/San_Antonio_River "San Antonio River") in the [San Pedro Springs](/wiki/San_Pedro_Springs "San Pedro Springs") area. They called the vicinity *[Yanaguana](/wiki/Yanaguana_(San_Antonio) "Yanaguana (San Antonio)")*, meaning "refreshing waters". In 1691, a group of Spanish explorers and [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") came upon the river and Payaya settlement on June 13, the feast day of [St. Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua").[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20) They named the place and river "San Antonio" in his honor.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Flag_of_the_United_States.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_States.svg.png) [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (1846–1861)
* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Flag_of_the_Confederate_States_%281863%E2%80%931865%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_Confederate_States_%281863%E2%80%931865%29.svg.png) [Confederate States](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") (1861–1865)
* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Flag_of_the_United_States.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_States.svg.png) [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (1865–present)

It was years before any Spanish settlement took place. [Father Antonio de Olivares](/wiki/Antonio_de_Olivares "Antonio de Olivares") visited the site in 1709, and he was determined to found a mission and civilian settlement there. The viceroy gave formal approval for a combined mission and presidio in late 1716, as he wanted to forestall any French expansion into the area from their colony of [*La Louisiane*](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") to the east, as well as prevent illegal trading with the Payaya. He directed [Martín de Alarcón](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_de_Alarc%C3%B3n "Martín de Alarcón"), the governor of [Coahuila y Tejas](/wiki/Coahuila_y_Tejas "Coahuila y Tejas"), to establish the mission complex. Differences between Alarcón and Olivares resulted in delays, and construction did not start until 1718.[[22]](#cite_note-tshaonline.org-22) Olivares built, with the help of the [Payaya](/wiki/Payaya_Indians "Payaya Indians") and the [Pastia people](/wiki/Pastia_people "Pastia people"), the [Misión de San Antonio de Valero (The Alamo)](/wiki/Alamo_Mission_in_San_Antonio "Alamo Mission in San Antonio"), the [Presidio San Antonio de Bexar](/wiki/Presidio_San_Antonio_de_Bexar "Presidio San Antonio de Bexar"), the bridge that connected both, and the [Acequia Madre de Valero](/wiki/Acequia_Madre_de_Valero_(San_Antonio) "Acequia Madre de Valero (San Antonio)").[[5]](#cite_note-Adina-5)

The families who clustered around the presidio and mission were the start of Villa de Béjar, destined to become the most important town in [Spanish Texas](/wiki/Spanish_Texas "Spanish Texas").[[23]](#cite_note-23) On May 1, the governor transferred ownership of the Mission San Antonio de Valero (later famous as The Alamo) to Fray Antonio de Olivares.[[5]](#cite_note-Adina-5) On May 5, 1718, he commissioned the Presidio San Antonio de Béxar ("Béjar" in modern Spanish orthography) on the west side of the San Antonio River, one-fourth league from the mission.[[22]](#cite_note-tshaonline.org-22)

On February 14, 1719, the Marquis of [San Miguel de Aguayo](/wiki/San_Miguel_de_Aguayo,_Texas "San Miguel de Aguayo, Texas") proposed to the king of Spain that 400 families be transported from the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)"), or [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana") to populate the province of Texas. His plan was approved, and notice was given the [Canary Islanders](/wiki/Canary_Islanders "Canary Islanders") (*[isleños](/wiki/Isle%C3%B1os "Isleños")*) to furnish 200 families; the [Council of the Indies](/wiki/Council_of_the_Indies "Council of the Indies") suggested that 400 families should be sent from the Canaries to Texas by way of Havana and [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz_(city) "Veracruz (city)"). By June 1730, 25 families had reached [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba_(island) "Cuba (island)"), and 10 families had been sent to Veracruz before orders from Spain came to stop the re-settlement.[[24]](#cite_note-TXHand-24)

Under the leadership of [Juan Leal Goraz](/wiki/Juan_Leal_Goraz "Juan Leal Goraz"), the group marched overland from Veracruz to the Presidio San Antonio de Béxar, where they arrived on March 9, 1731. Due to marriages along the way, the party now included 15 families, a total of 56 persons. They joined the military community established in 1718. The immigrants formed the nucleus of the villa of San Fernando de Béxar, the first regularly organized civil government in Texas. Several older families of San Antonio trace their descent from the Canary Island colonists. María Rosa Padrón was the first baby born of Canary Islander descent in San Antonio.[[24]](#cite_note-TXHand-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain_Location_1819_%28without_Philippines%29.png/250px-Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain_Location_1819_%28without_Philippines%29.png)](/wiki/File:Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain_Location_1819_(without_Philippines).png)

San Antonio was part of the Spanish [Viceroyalty of New Spain](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain "Viceroyalty of New Spain").

During the Spanish–Mexican settlement of Southwestern lands, which took place over the following century, Juan Leal Goraz Jr. was a prominent figure. He claimed nearly 100,000 square miles (64,000,000 acres; 260,000 km2) as Spanish territory and held some control for nearly three decades; this area stretched across six present-day states. San Antonio was designated as Leal Goraz's capital. It represented Mexican expansion into the area. With his robust military forces, he led exploration and establishing Spanish colonial bases as far as [San Francisco, California](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). Widespread bankruptcy forced Leal Goraz Jr.'s army back into the current boundaries of Mexico; they fell into internal conflict and turmoil with neighboring entities.

San Antonio grew to become the largest Spanish settlement in Texas; it was designated as the capital of the Spanish, later [Mexican, province of Tejas](/wiki/Mexican_Texas "Mexican Texas"). From San Antonio, the [Camino Real](/wiki/Old_San_Antonio_Road "Old San Antonio Road") (today Nacogdoches Road), was built to the small frontier town of [Nacogdoches](/wiki/Nacogdoches "Nacogdoches"). Mexico allowed [European American](/wiki/European_Americans "European Americans") settlers from the United States into the territory; they mostly occupied land in the eastern part. In 1835, when [Antonio López de Santa Anna](/wiki/Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Antonio López de Santa Anna") unilaterally abolished the [Mexican Constitution of 1824](/wiki/1824_Constitution_of_Mexico "1824 Constitution of Mexico"), violence ensued in many [states of Mexico](/wiki/States_of_Mexico "States of Mexico"). which led to many short-lived independent republics.[[25]](#cite_note-25) This, in addition to Mexico's abolition of slavery, and cultural differences between the Texians and the Mexicans, led to the [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In a series of battles, the [Texian Army](/wiki/Texian_Army "Texian Army"), which was made up primarily of American citizens, succeeded in forcing Mexican soldiers out of the settlement areas east of San Antonio. Under the leadership of [Ben Milam](/wiki/Benjamin_Milam "Benjamin Milam"), in the [Battle of Bexar](/wiki/Siege_of_B%C3%A9xar#Battle "Siege of Béxar"), December 1835, [Texian](/wiki/Texian "Texian") forces captured San Antonio from forces commanded by General [Martin Perfecto de Cos](/wiki/Martin_Perfecto_de_Cos "Martin Perfecto de Cos"), Santa Anna's brother-in-law. In the spring of 1836, Santa Anna marched on San Antonio. A volunteer force under the command of [James C. Neill](/wiki/James_C._Neill "James C. Neill") occupied and fortified the deserted Alamo mission.[[28]](#cite_note-Papers-28)

Upon his departure, the joint command of [William Barrett Travis](/wiki/William_B._Travis "William B. Travis") and [James Bowie](/wiki/James_Bowie "James Bowie") were left in charge of defending the old mission. The [Battle of the Alamo](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Alamo "Battle of the Alamo") took place from February 23 to March 6, 1836. The outnumbered Texian force was ultimately defeated, with all of the Alamo defenders killed. These men were seen as "martyrs" for the cause of Texas freedom and "Remember the Alamo" became a rallying cry in the Texian Army's eventual success at defeating Santa Anna's army.[[28]](#cite_note-Papers-28)

[Juan Seguín](/wiki/Juan_Segu%C3%ADn "Juan Seguín"), who organized the company of [Tejano](/wiki/Tejano "Tejano") patriots, who fought for Texas independence, fought at the [Battle of Concepción](/wiki/Battle_of_Concepci%C3%B3n "Battle of Concepción"), the [Siege of Bexar](/wiki/Siege_of_Bexar "Siege of Bexar"), and the [Battle of San Jacinto](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Jacinto "Battle of San Jacinto"), and served as mayor of San Antonio. He was forced out of office due to threats on his life by sectarian newcomers and political opponents in 1842 and was the last Tejano mayor for nearly 150 years.[[29]](#cite_note-29) By 1850, Tejanos no longer constituted a majority of the San Antonio population, and would not resume majority status until 1968.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/San_Antonio%2C_Texas_in_1886.jpg/250px-San_Antonio%2C_Texas_in_1886.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio,_Texas_in_1886.jpg)

Lithograph of San Antonio in 1886

In 1845, the United States finally decided to annex Texas and include it as a state in the Union. This led to the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"). Though the U.S. ultimately won, the war was devastating to San Antonio. By its end, the population of the city had been reduced by almost two-thirds, to 800 inhabitants.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Bolstered by migrants and immigrants, by 1860 at the start of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), San Antonio had grown to a city of 15,000 people.

In the 1850s, [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted"), the landscape architect who designed [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") in New York City, traveled throughout the Southern and [Southwest](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") U.S., and published accounts of his observations. In his 1859 book about Texas, Olmsted described San Antonio as having a "jumble of races, costumes, languages, and buildings", which gave it a quality that only [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") could rival in what he described as "odd and antiquated foreignness."[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Following the Civil War, San Antonio prospered as a center of the cattle industry. During this period, it remained a frontier city, with a mixture of cultures that was different from other U.S. cities.

German immigrants founded smaller surrounding towns such as [New Braunfels](/wiki/New_Braunfels,_Texas "New Braunfels, Texas"), [Castroville](/wiki/Castroville,_Texas "Castroville, Texas"), [Boerne](/wiki/Boerne,_Texas "Boerne, Texas"), [Comfort](/wiki/Comfort,_Texas "Comfort, Texas"), [Fredericksburg](/wiki/Fredericksburg,_Texas "Fredericksburg, Texas"), and [Bulverde](/wiki/Bulverde,_Texas "Bulverde, Texas"), all towns far out from San Antonio. However, the Germans were then drawn to San Antonio for work, and many buildings and streets still bear German names such as Wurzbach, Huebner, and Jones Maltsberger, and Wiederstein. The German impact on San Antonio was great, in the early 1900s it is estimated that at least 1/3 of San Antonio was ethnically German. Many descendants of German immigrants in San Antonio spoke [Texas German](/wiki/Texas_German "Texas German") up to the fifth or sixth generations. Texas German is a dialect of German that evolved when the German language was separated from Germany. Texas German is best described as an anglicized-German dialect with a Texas twang. Many older generations in New Braunfels and Fredericksburg still speak Texas German to this day.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In 1877, following the [Reconstruction Era](/wiki/Reconstruction_Era "Reconstruction Era"), the [Galveston, Harrisburg, & San Antonio Railroad](/wiki/Galveston,_Harrisburg,_%26_San_Antonio_Railroad "Galveston, Harrisburg, & San Antonio Railroad") became the first [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad") to reach San Antonio, connecting it to major markets and port cities.[[35]](#cite_note-TSHA_GHSA-35) Texas was the first state to have major cities develop by railroads rather than waterways.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In Texas, the railroads supported a markedly different pattern of development of major interior cities, such as San Antonio, [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") and [Fort Worth](/wiki/Fort_Worth "Fort Worth"), compared to the historical development of coastal port cities in the established eastern states.

At the beginning of the 20th century, the streets of the city's downtown were widened to accommodate street cars and modern traffic. At that time, many of the older historic buildings were demolished in the process of this modernization.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Since the late twentieth century, San Antonio has had steady [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth"). The city's population has nearly doubled in 35 years, from just over 650,000 in the 1970 census to an estimated 1.2 million in 2005, through both population growth and land annexation (the latter has considerably enlarged the physical area of the city).[[38]](#cite_note-38) In 1990, the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") reported San Antonio's population as 55.6% Hispanic or Latino, 7.0% Black or African American, and 36.2% non-Hispanic white.[[39]](#cite_note-census1-39)

The San Antonio Missions National Historical Park and The Alamo became [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") World Heritage sites in 2015 and the city was designated a UNESCO "City of Creativity for Gastronomy" in 2017, one of only 26 [gastronomy](/wiki/Gastronomy "Gastronomy") creative cities in the world.[[18]](#cite_note-PBS-SanAn-18)

With the increase in professional jobs, San Antonio has become a destination for many college-educated persons, most recently including African Americans in a [reverse Great Migration](/wiki/New_Great_Migration "New Great Migration") from northern and western areas.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Over 31,000 migrants who requested asylum have been released by the Border Patrol into the city in 2019 during the [National Emergency Concerning the Southern Border of the United States](/wiki/National_Emergency_Concerning_the_Southern_Border_of_the_United_States "National Emergency Concerning the Southern Border of the United States").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/San_Antonio_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-10-01.jpg/250px-San_Antonio_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-10-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-10-01.jpg)

Satellite picture of San Antonio by ESA Sentinel-2

San Antonio is approximately 75 miles (121 km) to the southwest of its neighboring city, [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), the [state capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States"), about 150 miles (240 km) north of the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"), about 190 miles (310 km) west of [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), and about 250 miles (400 km) south of the [Dallas–Fort Worth metropolitan area](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"). The city has a total area of 465.4 square miles (1,205.4 km2); 460.93 square miles (1,193.8 km2) of San Antonio's total area is land and 4.5 square miles (11.7 km2) of it is water.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The city's gently rolling terrain is dotted with oak trees, forested land, mesquite, and cacti. The [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country") reaches into the far northern portions of the city. San Antonio sits on the [Balcones Escarpment](/wiki/Balcones_Escarpment "Balcones Escarpment"). Its altitude is approximately 662 feet (202 m) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The city's primary source of drinking water is the [Edwards Aquifer](/wiki/Edwards_Aquifer "Edwards Aquifer").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Impounded in 1962 and 1969, respectively, [Victor Braunig Lake](/wiki/Victor_Braunig_Lake "Victor Braunig Lake") and [Calaveras Lake](/wiki/Calaveras_Lake_(Texas) "Calaveras Lake (Texas)") were among the first [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir_(water) "Reservoir (water)") in the United States built to use recycled treated wastewater for [power plant](/wiki/Power_plant "Power plant") cooling, reducing the amount of groundwater needed for [electrical](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") generation.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

Further information: [Neighborhoods and districts of San Antonio](/wiki/Neighborhoods_and_districts_of_San_Antonio "Neighborhoods and districts of San Antonio") and [Downtown San Antonio](/wiki/Downtown_San_Antonio "Downtown San Antonio")

#### Downtown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Downtown")]

Downtown San Antonio, the city and metro area's urban core, encompasses many of the city's famous structures, attractions, and businesses. The central business district is generally understood to cover the northern half of the "Downtown Loop"—the area bordered by Cesar Chavez to the south. Due to the size of the city and its horizontal development, downtown accounts for less than one half of one percent of San Antonio's geographic area.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The Strip (north of Downtown) houses a concentration of clubs and bars catering to the [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") community.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### North Central

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: North Central")]

North Central is home to several [enclaves](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") and upscale neighborhoods including [Castle Hills](/wiki/Castle_Hills,_Texas "Castle Hills, Texas"), Shavano Park, [Hollywood Park](/wiki/Hollywood_Park,_Texas "Hollywood Park, Texas"), Elm Creek, [Inwood](/wiki/Inwood,_San_Antonio "Inwood, San Antonio"), [Stone Oak](/wiki/Stone_Oak,_San_Antonio "Stone Oak, San Antonio"), and Rogers Ranch. The area is also the location of upper-middle-class neighborhoods (Deerfield, Churchill Estates, Hunter's Creek, Oak Meadow, and Summerfield).

#### Northwest Side

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Northwest Side")]

Northwest Side is the location of the main campus of the [University of Texas at San Antonio](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas at San Antonio"), the [University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Health_Science_Center_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio"), and the Northwest Campus of the [University of the Incarnate Word](/wiki/University_of_the_Incarnate_Word "University of the Incarnate Word"), which includes the Rosenberg School of Optometry. The Medical Center District is also located in Northwest Side. Companies with headquarters in the area include [Valero](/wiki/Valero_Energy "Valero Energy") and [NuStar Energy](/wiki/NuStar_Energy "NuStar Energy").

#### South Side

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: South Side")]

The South Side area of San Antonio is characterized by its predominantly [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") neighborhoods, who on average, account for about 65 percent of the population.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Large growth came to the South Side when [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") constructed a [manufacturing plant](/wiki/Toyota_Motor_Manufacturing_Texas "Toyota Motor Manufacturing Texas"). [Palo Alto College](/wiki/Palo_Alto_College "Palo Alto College") and [Texas A&M University–San Antonio](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University%E2%80%93San_Antonio "Texas A&M University–San Antonio") are also located in the area.

#### East Side

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: East Side")]

Further information: [History of African Americans in San Antonio](/wiki/History_of_African_Americans_in_San_Antonio "History of African Americans in San Antonio")

The East Side of San Antonio is home to the [San Antonio Stock Show & Rodeo](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stock_Show_%26_Rodeo "San Antonio Stock Show & Rodeo"), the [Frost Bank Center](/wiki/Frost_Bank_Center "Frost Bank Center"), and the [Freeman Coliseum](/wiki/Freeman_Coliseum "Freeman Coliseum"). This area is plagued with high crime, and has the largest concentration of [Black and African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") residents.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

#### West Side

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: West Side")]

The West Side is predominantly Hispanic/Latin American and working class, with pockets of wealth in the northwest and far west. African Americans are also located in parts of San Antonio's West Side.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The West Side has undergone gentrification as of 2019.[[53]](#cite_note-53) It includes the diverse neighborhoods of Avenida Guadalupe, Collins Garden, Las Palmas, Prospect Hill, Rainbow Hills (Marbach), San Juan Gardens, Loma Park, Loma Vista, Memorial Heights, and Westwood. It is also home to the historic [Our Lady of the Lake University](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_the_Lake_University "Our Lady of the Lake University") and [St. Mary's University](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_University,_Texas "St. Mary's University, Texas").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Downtown_San_Antonio_Skyline_Panorama.jpg/960px-Downtown_San_Antonio_Skyline_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_San_Antonio_Skyline_Panorama.jpg "Skyline of San Antonio (2013)")

Skyline of San Antonio (2013)

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

Natural vegetation in the San Antonio area, where undisturbed by development, includes oak-cedar woodland, oak grassland savanna, chaparral brush, and riparian (stream) woodland. San Antonio is at the westernmost limit for both Cabbage palmetto (*[Sabal palmetto](/wiki/Sabal_palmetto "Sabal palmetto")*) and [Spanish moss](/wiki/Spanish_moss "Spanish moss").

The native Eastern Subterranean Termite (*[Reticulitermes flavipes](/wiki/Reticulitermes_flavipes "Reticulitermes flavipes")*) is a common [structural pest](/wiki/Structural_pest "Structural pest") here.[[54]](#cite_note-Baiting-54) The Formosan Termite (*[Coptotermes formosanus](/wiki/Coptotermes_formosanus "Coptotermes formosanus")*) is an [invasive pest](/wiki/Invasive_pest "Invasive pest") originally from the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East").[[55]](#cite_note-TAM-Formosa-55)[[56]](#cite_note-Austin-56) Researchers at [Texas A&M University](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University "Texas A&M University") consider it to be economically devastating.[[55]](#cite_note-TAM-Formosa-55)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

San Antonio has a transitional [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfa*) that borders a [hot semi-arid climate](/wiki/Hot_semi-arid_climate "Hot semi-arid climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *BSh*) towards the west of the city[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) featuring very hot, long, and humid summers and mild winters. The area is subject to descending northern cold fronts in the winter with cool to cold nights, and is warm and rainy in the spring and fall. San Antonio falls in [USDA](/wiki/USDA "USDA") [hardiness zones](/wiki/Hardiness_zones "Hardiness zones") 8b (15 °F to 20 °F) and 9a (20 °F to 25 °F).[[60]](#cite_note-60)

San Antonio receives about a dozen subfreezing nights each year, typically seeing snow, sleet, or freezing rain about once every two or three winters, but accumulation and snow itself are very rare. Winters may pass without any frozen precipitation at all, and up to a decade has passed between snowfalls. According to the [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service"), there have been 32 instances of snowfall (a trace or more) in the city in the past 122 years, about once every four years. Prior to 2021 snow was most recently seen on December 7, 2017, when 1.9 inches (4.8 cm) of snow coated the city.[[61]](#cite_note-61) On January 13, 1985, San Antonio received a record snowfall of 16 inches (41 cm).[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) During the [February 13–17, 2021, North American winter storm](/wiki/February_13%E2%80%9317,_2021,_North_American_winter_storm "February 13–17, 2021, North American winter storm"), San Antonio was blanketed with 4–6 inches (10–15 cm) of snow. The cold air which accompanied this storm caused [massive rolling blackouts](/wiki/2021_Texas_power_crisis "2021 Texas power crisis") throughout the city until the 18th. The [February 15–20, 2021, North American winter storm](/wiki/February_15%E2%80%9320,_2021,_North_American_winter_storm "February 15–20, 2021, North American winter storm") dropped another 1–2 inches (2.5–5 cm) on the city on the 16th.

San Antonio and [New Braunfels](/wiki/New_Braunfels "New Braunfels"), 40 miles (64 km) to the northeast, are some of the most flood-prone regions in North America.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The [October 1998 Central Texas floods](/wiki/October_1998_Central_Texas_floods "October 1998 Central Texas floods") were among the costliest floods in United States history, resulting in $750 million in damage and 32 deaths. In 2002, from June 30 to July 7, 35 in (890 mm) of rain fell in the San Antonio area, resulting in widespread flooding and 12 fatalities.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Tornadoes within the city limits have been reported as recently as February 2017, although they are uncommon.[[66]](#cite_note-66) An F2 tornado lands within 50 mi (80 km) of the city on average once every five years. San Antonio has experienced two F4 tornadoes, [one in 1953](/wiki/Tornado_outbreak_sequence_of_April_28_%E2%80%93_May_2,_1953 "Tornado outbreak sequence of April 28 – May 2, 1953") and another in 1973. The 1953 tornado resulted in two deaths and 15 injuries.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Based on historical data, San Antonio is the safest major city in Texas when it comes to tornadoes.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In San Antonio, July and August tie for the average warmest months, with an average high of 95 °F (35 °C). The highest temperature ever recorded was 111 °F (44 °C) on September 5, 2000.[[69]](#cite_note-climate-summary-69) The average coolest month is January. The lowest recorded temperature ever was 0 °F (−18 °C) on January 31, 1949.[[69]](#cite_note-climate-summary-69) May, June, and October have quite a bit of precipitation. Since recordkeeping began in 1871, the average annual precipitation has been 29.03 inches (737 mm), with a maximum of 52.28 inches (1,328 mm) and a minimum of 10.11 inches (256.8 mm) in one year.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_San_Antonio_%285559903509%29.png/250px-Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_San_Antonio_%285559903509%29.png)](/wiki/File:Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_San_Antonio_(5559903509).png)

Map of racial distribution in San Antonio, 2010 U.S. census. Each dot is 25 people: ⬤ White ⬤ Black ⬤ Asian ⬤ Hispanic ⬤ Other

The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")'s 2020 census determined San Antonio had a population of 1,434,625 residents in 2020. In 2019, the [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimated San Antonio had a racial makeup of 88.4% White, 6.6% Black and African American, 0.2% American Indian and Alaska Native, 2.8% Asian, 0.1% Native Hawaiian and other Pacific Islander, 0.2% some other race and 1.7% two or more races. Ethnically, 64.5% were Hispanic or Latin American of any race.[[76]](#cite_note-:5-78) In 2020, its racial and ethnic makeup was 23.4% non-Hispanic white, 63.9% Hispanic or Latin American of any race, 6.5% Black and African American, 3.2% Asian, and 2.3% multiracial or some other race.

According to the [2010 U.S. census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), 1,327,407 people resided in San Antonio city proper, an increase of 16.0% since 2000. The racial composition of the city based on the 2010 U.S. census is as follows: 72.6% [White](/wiki/White_American "White American") ([non-Hispanic whites](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_Whites "Non-Hispanic Whites"): 26.6%), 6.9% Black or [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 0.9% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 2.4% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.1% [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") or [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"), 3.4% [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"), and 13.7% [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"). In addition, 63.2% of the city's population was of [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") origin, of any race.[[75]](#cite_note-census2-77) According to the [2000 U.S. census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), the city proper had a population of 1,144,646,[[77]](#cite_note-79) ranking it the ninth-most populated city in the country. San Antonio has a relatively lower cost of living compared to most Texas and U.S. major cities and a strong job market.[[78]](#cite_note-80) However, due to San Antonio's low density and relatively small suburban population, the metropolitan area ranked just 30th in the United States, with a population of 1,592,383 in 2000.[[79]](#cite_note-auto-81) San Antonio has a large Hispanic population with a significant African American population.[[79]](#cite_note-auto-81)

The 2011 U.S. census estimate for the eight-county [San Antonio–New Braunfels](/wiki/Greater_San_Antonio "Greater San Antonio") metropolitan area placed its population at 2,194,927.[[80]](#cite_note-82) The 2017 estimate for Greater San Antonio was 2,473,974, making it the third-most populous metro area in Texas (after the [Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex") and [Greater Houston](/wiki/Greater_Houston "Greater Houston")) and the 24th-most populous metro area in the U.S.[[81]](#cite_note-CensBur-83)

About 405,474 households, and 280,993 families resided in San Antonio. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") as of 2010 was 2,808.5 people per square mile (1,084.4 people/km2). There were 433,122 housing units at an average density of 1,062.7 per square mile (410.3/km2). The age of the city's population was distributed as 28.5% under the age of 18, 10.8% from 18 to 24, 30.8% from 25 to 44, 19.4% from 45 to 64, and 10.4% who are 65 years of age or older. The median age was 32 years. In San Antonio, 48% of the population were males, and 52% of the population were females. For every 100 females, there were 93.5 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 89.7 males.

At the 2019 American Community Survey, there were 512,273 households and 319,673 families.[[82]](#cite_note-84) The average household size was 2.98 and the average family size was 3.83. Of the local population,[[83]](#cite_note-85) 201,960 were married-couple households and 172,741 were female households with no spouse or partner present. An estimated 85,462 households were single-person. Roughly 218,249 residents in San Antonio were foreign-born residents. For every 100 females, San Antonio had 97.1 males.[[76]](#cite_note-:5-78)

At the 2010 U.S. census, San Antonio's median income for a household was $36,214, and the median income for a family was $53,100. Males have a median income of $30,061 versus $24,444 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city is $17,487. About 17.3% of the population and 14.0% of families are below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"). Of the total population, 24.3% of those under the age of 18 and 13.5% of those 65 and older were living below the poverty line.[[84]](#cite_note-86) In 2019, households had a median income of $53,571 and a mean income of $72,587.[[85]](#cite_note-87) An estimated 16.8% of the population lived at or below the poverty line.[[86]](#cite_note-88) The city of San Antonio and its metropolis was rated the poorest in 2019.[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91)

A [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)") study in 2015 determined 4% of the city and Greater San Antonio identified as [lesbian, gay, bisexual, or transgender](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT").[[90]](#cite_note-:6-92) In 2016, San Antonio scored a 90 out of 100 in its treatment of the LGBT community.[[91]](#cite_note-93)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/San_Fernando_Cathedral.jpg/250px-San_Fernando_Cathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Fernando_Cathedral.jpg)

[San Fernando Cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral_of_San_Fernando_(San_Antonio) "Cathedral of San Fernando (San Antonio)") is the see of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Antonio.

The population of Greater San Antonio is predominantly [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"). Owing in part to San Antonio's [Spanish and Mexican heritage](/wiki/Religion_in_Latin_America "Religion in Latin America"), [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") is the largest religious group in the region.[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-:01-95) In addition, American missionary work and immigration into Texas have also resulted in a substantial [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") population.[[94]](#cite_note-96)

The Catholic population forms the largest Christian group in the city and Greater San Antonio.[[93]](#cite_note-:01-95) San Antonian Catholics are served primarily by the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Antonio](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_San_Antonio "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Antonio"). The [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church")'s Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Antonio was established on August 27, 1784, under the then [Diocese of Galveston](/wiki/Diocese_of_Galveston "Diocese of Galveston").[[95]](#cite_note-97) It was elevated to archdiocese status in 1926.

According to [Sperling's BestPlaces](/wiki/Sperling%27s_BestPlaces "Sperling's BestPlaces") in 2020, the second largest Christian group were [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"). The largest Baptist Christian denominations within San Antonio and its metro area were the [Baptist General Convention of Texas](/wiki/Baptist_General_Convention_of_Texas "Baptist General Convention of Texas"),[[96]](#cite_note-98) the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention"),[[97]](#cite_note-99) and the [Cooperative Baptist Fellowship](/wiki/Cooperative_Baptist_Fellowship "Cooperative Baptist Fellowship").[[98]](#cite_note-100) [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") formed the second largest Protestant group and the third largest Christian group for the area. The [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") was the most prominent Methodist denomination.[[99]](#cite_note-101) From 2017 to 2020, [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") outgrew [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and tied with the [Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") as the fourth largest Christian group. A major predominantly [African American-led church](/wiki/Black_Church_(African_American) "Black Church (African American)") is [Denver Heights](/wiki/Denver_Heights_COGIC "Denver Heights COGIC"), affiliated with the [Church of God in Christ](/wiki/Church_of_God_in_Christ "Church of God in Christ").

After Lutherans, [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") were the next largest Christian denomination, followed by [Episcopalian or Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), and Christians of other traditions including the Eastern Orthodox and [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"). The [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") communities are divided between the [Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Archdiocese_of_America "Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America"),[[100]](#cite_note-102) the [Antiochian Orthodox Archdiocese of North America](/wiki/Antiochian_Orthodox_Christian_Archdiocese_of_North_America "Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America"),[[101]](#cite_note-103) the [Orthodox Church in America](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_America "Orthodox Church in America"),[[102]](#cite_note-104) and the [Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church_Outside_Russia "Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia").[[103]](#cite_note-105) Episcopalians and Anglicans primarily are served by the [Episcopal Church in the United States](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") and the [Anglican Church in North America](/wiki/Anglican_Church_in_North_America "Anglican Church in North America").[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107) Churches affiliated with the Episcopal Church form the [Episcopal Diocese of West Texas](/wiki/Episcopal_Diocese_of_West_Texas "Episcopal Diocese of West Texas"). Overall, the Protestant demographic was predominantly [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") as of 2020.

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the second largest religion in the Greater San Antonio area.[[93]](#cite_note-:01-95) [Eastern religions](/wiki/Eastern_religions "Eastern religions") such as [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") also have a significant presence in San Antonio.[[106]](#cite_note-108) An estimated 0.3% of the area's population identified with [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") according to Sperling's BestPlaces and at least 10,000 Jews live in the city.[[107]](#cite_note-109) The San Antonio Jewish community began not long after the independence of the [Republic of Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas").[[108]](#cite_note-110) The oldest synagogue in [South Texas](/wiki/South_Texas "South Texas") ([Temple Beth-El](/wiki/Temple_Beth-El_(San_Antonio) "Temple Beth-El (San Antonio)")) is located in the city limits and located near [San Antonio College](/wiki/San_Antonio_College "San Antonio College").[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[110]](#cite_note-112)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Crime")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/San_Antonio_Police_Officers.jpg/250px-San_Antonio_Police_Officers.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio_Police_Officers.jpg)

San Antonio police officers

Crime in San Antonio began to rise in the early 1980s, similar to many urban areas in the US. In 1983 San Antonio had the tenth highest homicide rate in Texas with 18.5 homicides per 100,000 residents.[[111]](#cite_note-113) The number of juveniles arrested in San Antonio for violent crimes tripled between 1987 and 1994, according to the Texas Law Enforcement Management and Administration Statistics Program. The number of youths arrested for unlawfully carrying firearms doubled over the same period.

In 1993, San Antonio earned the nickname the "Drive-By City" after the [San Antonio Police Department](/wiki/San_Antonio_Police_Department "San Antonio Police Department") recorded over 1,200 drive-by shootings, or an average of about 3.5 per day, which overshadowed the number in other Texas cities. A majority of the violence occurred on the east and west sides of the city, especially in areas with high poverty rates. Gang violence led to the deaths of their members and sometimes innocent bystanders, and housing projects such as the [Alazán-Apache Courts](/wiki/Alaz%C3%A1n-Apache_Courts "Alazán-Apache Courts") served as hubs for various groups, which sometimes included rival gangs.[[112]](#cite_note-114) By the end of 1993 the city hit a peak in homicides with 230 killings, the highest since 1991 when 211 were killed.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

In 2016, the number of murders hit 151, the highest toll in 20 years. A majority of the San Antonio homicide victims were Hispanic and African American men between ages 18 and 29. According to a study, 40% of the killings were either drug-related or domestic incidents.[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-117) In 2020, San Antonio ranked the fourth U.S. city with the biggest increase in homicides.[[116]](#cite_note-118) From January to June 2020, there were 71 homicides according to the San Antonio Police Department. In 2019, there were 53 reported homicides in contrast. A total of 105 homicides occurred in 2019 in the city. Redevelopment, harmonious Black and Hispanic relations, improved police patrol, community-based peace programs, gang intervention work, and youth development organizations lowered the murder and crime rates in San Antonio to levels that had not been seen since the 1940s and 1950s.[[117]](#cite_note-119)[[118]](#cite_note-120) According to *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, homicide rates were relatively low compared to previous decades.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economy")]

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **SAT** |  | **Corporation** |  | **US** |
| 1 |  | [Valero Energy Corp.](/wiki/Valero_Energy_Corp. "Valero Energy Corp.") |  | 29 |
| 2 |  | [USAA](/wiki/USAA "USAA") |  | 103 |
|  |  |  |  |  |

San Antonio has a diversified economy with a gross domestic product (GDP) of approximately $121 billion in 2018.[[120]](#cite_note-122) San Antonio's economy focuses primarily on [military](/wiki/Military "Military"), [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care"), government–[civil service](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service"), professional and business services,[[121]](#cite_note-123) [oil and gas](/wiki/Oil_and_gas "Oil and gas"), and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"). Since the beginning of the 21st century, the city has become a significant location for American-based [call centers](/wiki/Call_centers "Call centers") and has added a sizable [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") sector centered around automobiles.[[122]](#cite_note-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125) The city also has a growing technology sector.[[124]](#cite_note-126) Located about 10 miles (16 km) northwest of [Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_San_Antonio "Downtown San Antonio") is the [South Texas Medical Center](/wiki/South_Texas_Medical_Center "South Texas Medical Center"), a conglomerate of various hospitals, clinics, and research (see [Southwest Research Institute](/wiki/Southwest_Research_Institute "Southwest Research Institute") and [Texas Biomedical Research Institute](/wiki/Texas_Biomedical_Research_Institute "Texas Biomedical Research Institute")) and higher educational institutions.[[125]](#cite_note-127)

Over twenty million tourists visit the city and its attractions every year, contributing substantially to its economy, primarily due to The Alamo and the [River Walk](/wiki/San_Antonio_River_Walk "San Antonio River Walk").[[126]](#cite_note-128) The [Henry B. Gonzalez Convention Center](/wiki/Henry_B._Gonzalez_Convention_Center "Henry B. Gonzalez Convention Center") alone hosts over 300 events annually with more than 750,000 convention delegates from around the world. Tourism provided over 130,000 jobs, and it had an economic impact of $13.6 billion in the local economy according to information gathered in 2017.[[127]](#cite_note-129) The city of San Antonio received $195 million in the same year from the hospitality industry, with revenues from hotel occupancy tax, sales taxes and others.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Valero_campus_%282%29.JPG/250px-Valero_campus_%282%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Valero_campus_(2).JPG)

Headquarters of [Valero Energy Corporation](/wiki/Valero_Energy_Corporation "Valero Energy Corporation")

San Antonio is the headquarters of two [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies: [Valero Energy](/wiki/Valero_Energy "Valero Energy") and [USAA](/wiki/USAA "USAA"). [iHeartMedia](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia") had been on the list but fell off,[[128]](#cite_note-130) and [NuStar Energy](/wiki/NuStar_Energy_L.P. "NuStar Energy L.P.") was also on the list until it was purchased by [Sunoco LP](/wiki/Sunoco "Sunoco") in 2024. [H-E-B](/wiki/H-E-B "H-E-B"), the 5th-largest private company in the U.S.,[[129]](#cite_note-131) is also headquartered in San Antonio.

Other companies headquartered in the city include [Bill Miller Bar-B-Q Enterprises](/wiki/Bill_Miller_Bar-B-Q_Enterprises "Bill Miller Bar-B-Q Enterprises"), [Carenet Health](/wiki/Carenet_Health "Carenet Health"), [Security Service Federal Credit Union](/wiki/Security_Service_Federal_Credit_Union "Security Service Federal Credit Union"), [Visionworks of America](/wiki/Visionworks_of_America "Visionworks of America"), [Frost Bank](/wiki/Frost_Bank "Frost Bank"), [Harte-Hanks](/wiki/Harte-Hanks "Harte-Hanks"), [Kinetic Concepts](/wiki/Kinetic_Concepts "Kinetic Concepts"), SWBC, [NewTek](/wiki/NewTek "NewTek"), [Rackspace](/wiki/Rackspace "Rackspace"), [Pabst Brewing Company](/wiki/Pabst_Brewing_Company "Pabst Brewing Company"), [Taco Cabana](/wiki/Taco_Cabana "Taco Cabana"), [Broadway Bank](/wiki/Broadway_Bank_(Texas) "Broadway Bank (Texas)"), [Zachry Holdings/Zachry Construction Company](/wiki/H.B._Zachry "H.B. Zachry"), [Randolph-Brooks Federal Credit Union](/wiki/Randolph-Brooks_Federal_Credit_Union "Randolph-Brooks Federal Credit Union"), [SAS](/wiki/SAS_(shoemakers) "SAS (shoemakers)"), [Globalscape](/wiki/Globalscape "Globalscape"), and [Whataburger](/wiki/Whataburger "Whataburger"). The [North American Development Bank](/wiki/North_American_Development_Bank "North American Development Bank"), a development finance institution jointly held by the governments of the U.S. and Mexico, is headquartered in San Antonio as well. Other notable companies that maintain sizable presences in the city include [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"),[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133) [OCI](/wiki/OCI_(company) "OCI (company)"),[[132]](#cite_note-134) [Capital Group](/wiki/Capital_Group_Companies "Capital Group Companies"), [CGI](/wiki/CGI_Inc. "CGI Inc."),[[133]](#cite_note-135)[[134]](#cite_note-136) [Marathon Petroleum](/wiki/Marathon_Petroleum "Marathon Petroleum"),[[135]](#cite_note-137) [Silver Spring Networks](/wiki/Silver_Spring_Networks "Silver Spring Networks"),[[136]](#cite_note-138)[[137]](#cite_note-139) [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota"),[[138]](#cite_note-140) [Argo Group](/wiki/Argo_Group "Argo Group"),[[139]](#cite_note-141) [EOG Resources](/wiki/EOG_Resources "EOG Resources"), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"),[[140]](#cite_note-142) [Cogeco Peer1](/wiki/Cogeco_Peer_1 "Cogeco Peer 1"),[[141]](#cite_note-143) [Wells Fargo](/wiki/Wells_Fargo "Wells Fargo"), [Citibank](/wiki/Citibank "Citibank"),[[142]](#cite_note-144) and [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing").[[143]](#cite_note-145) In December 2020, [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") announced plans to for three new facilities in San Antonio.[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147)

San Antonio has lost several major company headquarters, the largest being the 2008 move of [AT&T Inc.](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T") to Dallas "to better serve customers and expand business in the future."[[146]](#cite_note-148) In 2019, [Andeavor](/wiki/Andeavor "Andeavor") (Formerly Tesoro) was acquired by [Marathon Petroleum](/wiki/Marathon_Petroleum "Marathon Petroleum"); this merger eliminated the company and the headquarters was moved to [Findlay, Ohio](/wiki/Findlay,_Ohio "Findlay, Ohio").[[147]](#cite_note-149) After a [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") buyout specialist purchased [Builders Square](/wiki/Builders_Square "Builders Square"), the company's operations were moved out of San Antonio.[[148]](#cite_note-150)

The city is home to one of the largest concentrations of military bases in the U.S., and has been nicknamed "Military City, USA". The city is home to several active military installations: [Lackland Air Force Base](/wiki/Lackland_Air_Force_Base "Lackland Air Force Base"), [Brooke Army Medical Center](/wiki/Brooke_Army_Medical_Center "Brooke Army Medical Center"), [Randolph Air Force Base](/wiki/Randolph_Air_Force_Base "Randolph Air Force Base"), and [Fort Sam Houston](/wiki/Fort_Sam_Houston "Fort Sam Houston").[[149]](#cite_note-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152)[[151]](#cite_note-:3-153)[[152]](#cite_note-154)

San Antonio and Mexico share strong economic ties and engage in important exchanges to the benefit of their business communities. To better strengthen these business and cultural ties, the City of San Antonio opened Casa San Antonio to act as the city's trade and cultural office in Mexico.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

The [Formosan termite](/wiki/Formosan_termite "Formosan termite") (*Coptotermes formosanus*) causes economic devastation in the region due to the [structural damage](/wiki/Structural_pest "Structural pest") it causes.[[55]](#cite_note-TAM-Formosa-55)[[56]](#cite_note-Austin-56) It is an [invasive pest](/wiki/Invasive_pest "Invasive pest") originally from the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East").[[55]](#cite_note-TAM-Formosa-55)[[56]](#cite_note-Austin-56) First found in the state in 1957, it has since spread into the greater San Antonio area, creating a problem for the region's pecan orchards.[[154]](#cite_note-formosa-156)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

Further information: [Culture of San Antonio](/wiki/Culture_of_San_Antonio "Culture of San Antonio")

San Antonio enjoys a vibrant culture which it is known for being a popular tourist destination.[[155]](#cite_note-157) The [Alamo Mission in San Antonio](/wiki/Alamo_Mission_in_San_Antonio "Alamo Mission in San Antonio") ("The Alamo"), located in Downtown, is Texas' top tourist attraction. Because of the mission, San Antonio is often called "Alamo City".[[156]](#cite_note-158)

The [River Walk](/wiki/San_Antonio_River_Walk "San Antonio River Walk"), which meanders through the Downtown area, is the city's second-most-visited attraction, giving it the additional nickname of "River City". Extended by an additional 13 miles (21 km) between 2009 and 2013, the landscaped walking and bike path line the San Antonio River from the "Museum Reach" beginning in Brackenridge Park through downtown, "Downtown Reach", past the Blue Star's "Eagleland" to the "Mission Reach" ending near Loop 410 South past [Mission Espada](/wiki/Mission_Espada "Mission Espada").[[157]](#cite_note-159)

Lined with numerous shops, bars, and restaurants, as well as the [Arneson River Theater](/wiki/Arneson_River_Theater "Arneson River Theater"), this attraction is transformed into a festival of lights during the Christmas and New Year holiday period (except for the Mission Reach), and is suffused with the local sounds of [folklorico](/wiki/Baile_Folklorico "Baile Folklorico") and [flamenco](/wiki/Flamenco "Flamenco") music during the summer, particularly during celebrations such as the [Fiesta Noche del Rio](/wiki/Fiesta_Noche_del_Rio "Fiesta Noche del Rio").

The Downtown area also features [San Fernando Cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral_of_San_Fernando_(San_Antonio) "Cathedral of San Fernando (San Antonio)"), [The Majestic Theatre](/wiki/The_Majestic_Theatre,_San_Antonio "The Majestic Theatre, San Antonio"), [Hemisfair](/wiki/HemisFair_%2768 "HemisFair '68") (home of the [Tower of the Americas](/wiki/Tower_of_the_Americas "Tower of the Americas"), and UTSA's [Institute of Texan Cultures](/wiki/Institute_of_Texan_Cultures "Institute of Texan Cultures")), [La Villita](/wiki/La_Villita "La Villita"), [Market Square](/wiki/Market_Square_(San_Antonio) "Market Square (San Antonio)"), the [Spanish Governor's Palace](/wiki/Spanish_Governor%27s_Palace "Spanish Governor's Palace"), and the historic [Menger Hotel](/wiki/Menger_Hotel "Menger Hotel"). The [Fairmount Hotel](/wiki/Fairmount_Hotel_(San_Antonio,_Texas) "Fairmount Hotel (San Antonio, Texas)"), built in 1906 and San Antonio's second oldest hotel, is in the *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* as one of the heaviest buildings ever moved intact.[[158]](#cite_note-160) It was placed in its new location, three blocks south of the Alamo, over four days in 1985, and cost $650,000 to move.

[SeaWorld](/wiki/SeaWorld_San_Antonio "SeaWorld San Antonio"), 16 miles (26 km) west of Downtown in the city's Westover Hills district, is one of the largest marine life parks in the world. The [San Antonio Zoo](/wiki/San_Antonio_Zoo "San Antonio Zoo") is in Brackenridge Park. The British company [Merlin Entertainments](/wiki/Merlin_Entertainments "Merlin Entertainments") developed an aquarium attraction and indoor counterpart to SeaWorld. It is inside the Shops at Rivercenter in Downtown San Antonio and is one of Merlin's [Sea Life Aquariums](/wiki/Sea_Life_Centres "Sea Life Centres"). The [San Antonio Aquarium](/wiki/San_Antonio_Aquarium "San Antonio Aquarium") features a number of interactive exhibits.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Six_Flags_San_Antonio_2019_v2.jpg/250px-Six_Flags_San_Antonio_2019_v2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Six_Flags_San_Antonio_2019_v2.jpg)

Aerial view of [Six Flags Fiesta Texas](/wiki/Six_Flags_Fiesta_Texas "Six Flags Fiesta Texas")

San Antonio is also home to several commercial amusement parks, including Six Flags Fiesta Texas and [Morgan's Wonderland](/wiki/Morgan%27s_Wonderland "Morgan's Wonderland"), a theme park for children with special needs.[[159]](#cite_note-161) Kiddie Park, featuring old-fashioned amusement rides for children, was established in 1925 and is the oldest children's amusement park in the U.S.[[160]](#cite_note-162)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/McNayArtMuseum.jpg/250px-McNayArtMuseum.jpg)](/wiki/File:McNayArtMuseum.jpg)

The McNay Art Museum

San Antonio is home to the first museum of [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") in [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), the [McNay Art Museum](/wiki/McNay_Art_Museum "McNay Art Museum").[[161]](#cite_note-163)[[162]](#cite_note-164) Other art institutions and museums include [ArtPace](/wiki/ArtPace "ArtPace"), [Blue Star Contemporary Art Center](/wiki/Blue_Star_Contemporary_Art_Center "Blue Star Contemporary Art Center"), the Briscoe Western Art Museum, Ruby City, [Buckhorn Saloon & Museum](/wiki/Buckhorn_Saloon_%26_Museum_(San_Antonio) "Buckhorn Saloon & Museum (San Antonio)") (where visitors can experience something of cowboy culture year round), [San Antonio Museum of Art](/wiki/San_Antonio_Museum_of_Art "San Antonio Museum of Art"), formerly the Lonestar Brewery, Say Sí (mentoring San Antonio artistic youth), the Southwest School of Art, Texas Rangers Museum, [Texas Transportation Museum](/wiki/Texas_Transportation_Museum "Texas Transportation Museum"), the [Witte Museum](/wiki/Witte_Museum "Witte Museum") and the DoSeum. An outdoor display at [North Star Mall](/wiki/North_Star_Mall "North Star Mall") features 40-foot-tall (12-meter) cowboy boots.

The city's five missions, the four in the [San Antonio Missions National Historical Park](/wiki/San_Antonio_Missions_National_Historical_Park "San Antonio Missions National Historical Park") plus The Alamo, were named UNESCO World Heritage sites on July 5, 2015.[[18]](#cite_note-PBS-SanAn-18) The San Antonio Missions became the 23rd U.S. site on the World Heritage list, which includes the Grand Canyon and the Statue of Liberty; it is the first site in Texas. The new Mission Reach of the River Walk was completed in 2013, and created over 15 miles (24 km) of biking, hiking, and paddling trails that connect the Missions to Downtown and the Broadway Corridor.[[163]](#cite_note-165)

San Antonio's Howard W. Peak Greenway Trail System is a big draw. It covers more than 82 miles (132 km) and has more than 50 trailheads.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

Other places of interest include the [San Antonio Botanical Garden](/wiki/San_Antonio_Botanical_Garden "San Antonio Botanical Garden"), [Brackenridge Park](/wiki/Brackenridge_Park "Brackenridge Park"), the [Japanese Tea Gardens](/wiki/San_Antonio_Japanese_Tea_Gardens "San Antonio Japanese Tea Gardens"), and the [Woodlawn Theater](/wiki/Woodlawn_Theatre "Woodlawn Theatre").

In 2015 work was authorized to begin on the restoration of the former [Hot Wells](/wiki/Hot_Wells_(San_Antonio,_Texas) "Hot Wells (San Antonio, Texas)") hotel, [spa](/wiki/Spa "Spa"), and [bathhouses](/wiki/Public_bathing "Public bathing") on the San Antonio River on the city's south side.[[165]](#cite_note-167)

### Annual events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Annual events")]

Yearly events, such as [Fiesta San Antonio](/wiki/Fiesta_San_Antonio "Fiesta San Antonio") (the city's signature event), Luminaria (a contemporary arts festival) and Fiesta Noche del Rio, add a variety of entertainment options to the area, as well as strengthening the economy. Fiesta San Antonio alone carries an annual economic impact of $340 million.[[166]](#cite_note-168) The city also plays host to the [San Antonio Film Festival](/wiki/San_Antonio_Film_Festival "San Antonio Film Festival") every summer in August.[[167]](#cite_note-169)
