### Professional sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Professional sports")]

The city's only top-level professional sports team is the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"). Previously the Spurs played at the [Alamodome](/wiki/Alamodome "Alamodome") (which was speculatively built in an attempt to lure a professional [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team to the region), and before that the [HemisFair Arena](/wiki/HemisFair_Arena "HemisFair Arena"). They moved into the SBC Center in 2002 (since renamed the [Frost Bank Center](/wiki/Frost_Bank_Center "Frost Bank Center")), built with public funds.

The San Antonio metropolitan area's smaller population has so far contributed to its lack of an [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), [MLB](/wiki/MLB "MLB"), [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"), or [MLS](/wiki/MLS "MLS") team. City officials are said to be attempting to lure the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") permanently to San Antonio. Former NFL Commissioner [Paul Tagliabue](/wiki/Paul_Tagliabue "Paul Tagliabue") stated San Antonio was successful in temporarily hosting the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") following [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina"), and that the city would be on the short list for any future NFL expansions.[[168]](#cite_note-171) The city has also hosted the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") and [Houston Oilers](/wiki/Houston_Oilers "Houston Oilers") preseason camps in the past, and the Cowboys practiced in San Antonio through 2011.[[169]](#cite_note-172) Cowboys owner [Jerry Jones](/wiki/Jerry_Jones "Jerry Jones") acknowledged his support for the city to become home to an NFL franchise.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

The San Antonio Clutch of [The Basketball League](/wiki/The_Basketball_League "The Basketball League") (TBL) play at [St. Mary's University](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_University,_Texas "St. Mary's University, Texas").

San Antonio is home to the [Double-A](/wiki/Double-A_(baseball) "Double-A (baseball)") [San Antonio Missions](/wiki/San_Antonio_Missions "San Antonio Missions"), who play at [Nelson Wolff Stadium](/wiki/Nelson_W._Wolff_Municipal_Stadium "Nelson W. Wolff Municipal Stadium") and are the [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") affiliate of the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres").[[171]](#cite_note-174)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Soccer_Bowl_2014_%2817154199041%29.jpg/250px-Soccer_Bowl_2014_%2817154199041%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soccer_Bowl_2014_(17154199041).jpg)

[Toyota Field](/wiki/Toyota_Field "Toyota Field") during the 2014 Soccer Bowl

San Antonio had a professional soccer franchise when the [San Antonio Thunder](/wiki/San_Antonio_Thunder "San Antonio Thunder") played two seasons in the [original NASL](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(1968%E2%80%9384) "North American Soccer League (1968–84)") during the 1975–1976 seasons. Professional soccer returned with the birth of the [San Antonio Scorpions](/wiki/San_Antonio_Scorpions "San Antonio Scorpions") of the [modern NASL](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(2011%E2%80%932017) "North American Soccer League (2011–2017)") in 2012. The Scorpions won the [2014 Soccer Bowl](/wiki/Soccer_Bowl_2014 "Soccer Bowl 2014"), the first soccer championship in city history. On December 22, 2015, it was announced that Toyota Field and S.T.A.R. Soccer Complex were sold to the City of San Antonio and Bexar County, a deal which was accompanied by an agreement for [Spurs Sports and Entertainment](/wiki/Spurs_Sports_and_Entertainment "Spurs Sports and Entertainment") to operate the facilities and field a team would play in the [United Soccer League Championship](/wiki/United_Soccer_League_Championship "United Soccer League Championship"). [San Antonio FC](/wiki/San_Antonio_FC "San Antonio FC") began play in the soccer-specific stadium, [Toyota Field](/wiki/Toyota_Field "Toyota Field"), in 2016, and won the 2022 league championship. As a result, the [San Antonio Scorpions](/wiki/San_Antonio_Scorpions "San Antonio Scorpions") franchise of the [NASL](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(2011%E2%80%932017) "North American Soccer League (2011–2017)") was shut down.

San Antonio has two [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") teams, the [Alamo City Rugby Football Club](/wiki/Alamo_City_Rugby_Football_Club "Alamo City Rugby Football Club"), and San Antonio Rugby Football Club.

The city has played host to a number of major and minor league football teams, primarily at [Alamo Stadium](/wiki/Alamo_Stadium "Alamo Stadium") and the Alamodome. The [San Antonio Gunslingers](/wiki/San_Antonio_Gunslingers_(indoor_football) "San Antonio Gunslingers (indoor football)") of the [United States Football League](/wiki/United_States_Football_League "United States Football League") and the [San Antonio Riders](/wiki/San_Antonio_Riders "San Antonio Riders") of the [World League of American Football](/wiki/NFL_Europe#World_League_of_American_Football "NFL Europe") played for two seasons each in the 1980s and 1990s, respectively; while the [San Antonio Wings](/wiki/San_Antonio_Wings "San Antonio Wings") of the [World Football League](/wiki/World_Football_League "World Football League") and the [San Antonio Texans](/wiki/San_Antonio_Texans "San Antonio Texans") of the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") each played a single season. In 2018, the [Alliance of American Football](/wiki/Alliance_of_American_Football "Alliance of American Football") announced that the [San Antonio Commanders](/wiki/San_Antonio_Commanders "San Antonio Commanders") would play in the city beginning in 2019.[[172]](#cite_note-175) The Commanders opened play at the Alamodome in February 2019. San Antonio was also home to the minor-league Toros of the [Texas Football League](/wiki/Texas_Football_League "Texas Football League") (later the [Continental Football League](/wiki/Continental_Football_League "Continental Football League"), then Trans-American Football League) from 1966 to 1971; and the minor-league Charros of the [American Football Association](/wiki/American_Football_Association_(1978-1983) "American Football Association (1978-1983)") from 1978 to 1981. In 2020, the [San Antonio Brahmas](/wiki/San_Antonio_Brahmas "San Antonio Brahmas") of the [UFL](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2024) "United Football League (2024)")'s XFL conference began play at the Alamodome. In October 2025, it was announced that the team was ceasing operations in the city.

The [Valero Texas Open](/wiki/Valero_Texas_Open "Valero Texas Open") is a professional golf tournament on the [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour") held at San Antonio since 1922. It has been played at [TPC San Antonio](/wiki/TPC_San_Antonio "TPC San Antonio") since 2010. Previous venues include the [Brackenridge Park Golf Course](/wiki/Brackenridge_Park_Golf_Course "Brackenridge Park Golf Course"), [La Cantera Golf Club](/wiki/La_Cantera_Golf_Club "La Cantera Golf Club") and [Pecan Valley Golf Club](/wiki/Pecan_Valley_Golf_Club "Pecan Valley Golf Club"); the latter also hosted the [1968 PGA Championship](/wiki/1968_PGA_Championship "1968 PGA Championship"). The [Alamo Ladies Classic](/wiki/Alamo_Ladies_Classic "Alamo Ladies Classic") was an [LPGA Tour](/wiki/LPGA_Tour "LPGA Tour") event held from 1960 to 1973.

The first [Rising Phoenix World Championships](/wiki/2015_Rising_Phoenix_World_Championships "2015 Rising Phoenix World Championships") was held at [Grand Hyatt](/wiki/Grand_Hyatt "Grand Hyatt"), San Antonio in 2015.

The city used to be home to the [San Antonio Stars](/wiki/San_Antonio_Stars "San Antonio Stars") [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") until the franchise was relocated in October 2017 to [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") to become the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces").[[173]](#cite_note-176)

The city used to be home to the [San Antonio Rampage](/wiki/San_Antonio_Rampage "San Antonio Rampage") [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team until the franchise was sold in February 2020 to the [Vegas Golden Knights](/wiki/Vegas_Golden_Knights "Vegas Golden Knights").[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### College sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: College sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/San_Antonio_May_2018_5_%28Alamodome%29.jpg/250px-San_Antonio_May_2018_5_%28Alamodome%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio_May_2018_5_(Alamodome).jpg)

The [Alamodome](/wiki/Alamodome "Alamodome"), home of the UFL's [Brahmas](/wiki/San_Antonio_Brahmas "San Antonio Brahmas") and the [UTSA Roadrunners](/wiki/UTSA_Roadrunners "UTSA Roadrunners")

The [University of Texas at San Antonio](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas at San Antonio") fields San Antonio's [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") athletic teams, known as the [UTSA Roadrunners](/wiki/UTSA_Roadrunners "UTSA Roadrunners"). The teams play in the [American Athletic Conference](/wiki/American_Athletic_Conference "American Athletic Conference"). The university added football in 2011, hiring former [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami") coach [Larry Coker](/wiki/Larry_Coker "Larry Coker") as its initial head coach. Roadrunner football began play in 2011, with a record of 4–6. UTSA set attendance records for both highest attendance at an inaugural game (56,743) and highest average attendance for a first year program (35,521).[[175]](#cite_note-178)[[176]](#cite_note-179) The Roadrunners moved to the [Western Athletic Conference](/wiki/Western_Athletic_Conference "Western Athletic Conference") in 2012, to [Conference USA](/wiki/Conference_USA "Conference USA") in 2013, and to the [American Athletic Conference](/wiki/American_Athletic_Conference "American Athletic Conference") in 2023.

The [University of the Incarnate Word](/wiki/University_of_the_Incarnate_Word "University of the Incarnate Word") (UIW) also fields a full slate of [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") athletic teams, known as the [Incarnate Word Cardinals](/wiki/Incarnate_Word_Cardinals "Incarnate Word Cardinals"). UIW's football team competes in the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (FCS) in the [Southland Conference](/wiki/Southland_Conference "Southland Conference"). Since 2018, UIW's football team has won three Southland Conference championships and has made three appearances in the FCS playoffs.

Trinity University fields all the typical collegiate sports, but at the [NCAA Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") level. Trinity competes in the [Southern Collegiate Athletic Conference](/wiki/Southern_Collegiate_Athletic_Conference "Southern Collegiate Athletic Conference") (SCAC). The Trinity baseball team won the 2016 Division III College World Series, one of six national team and 21 national individual championships won by the school's athletic program in the Division III era.[[177]](#cite_note-trinitytigers.com-180) Prior to moving to Division III, Trinity was a national power in tennis, winning five USTA women's championships and one NCAA men's title between 1968 and 1976.[[177]](#cite_note-trinitytigers.com-180) [Chuck McKinley](/wiki/Chuck_McKinley "Chuck McKinley") won the men's championship at [Wimbledon](/wiki/The_Championships,_Wimbledon "The Championships, Wimbledon") and was a member of the winning [Davis Cup](/wiki/Davis_Cup "Davis Cup") team as a student at Trinity in 1963.[[178]](#cite_note-181)

San Antonio hosts the NCAA football [Alamo Bowl](/wiki/Alamo_Bowl "Alamo Bowl") each December. The city is also home of the U.S. Army All-American Bowl,[[179]](#cite_note-182) played annually in the Alamodome and televised live on NBC. The Bowl is an East versus West showdown featuring the nation's top 90 [high school football](/wiki/High_school_football "High school football") senior players. The game has featured NFL stars [Reggie Bush](/wiki/Reggie_Bush "Reggie Bush"), [Vince Young](/wiki/Vince_Young "Vince Young"), [Adrian Peterson](/wiki/Adrian_Peterson "Adrian Peterson"), and many other college and NFL stars.

The University of Texas at San Antonio fields the only collegiate men's rugby team in the city. UTSA competes in Division III Texas Rugby Union.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Government")]

Further information: [List of mayors of San Antonio](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_San_Antonio "List of mayors of San Antonio")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/City_Hall%2C_San_Antonio%2C_Texas_%281906%29.jpg/250px-City_Hall%2C_San_Antonio%2C_Texas_%281906%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Hall,_San_Antonio,_Texas_(1906).jpg)

City Hall, San Antonio, Texas (postcard, c. 1906)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/San_Antonio_City_Hall.jpg/250px-San_Antonio_City_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio_City_Hall.jpg)

San Antonio City Hall

The city of San Antonio is operated under the [council-manager](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government") system of government. The city is divided into 10 council districts designed to be of equal population. Each district elects one person to the city council, with the mayor elected on a citywide basis. All members of the San Antonio City Council, including the mayor, are elected to four-year terms and are limited to two terms (except for those who were in office in November 2008 and are limited to a total of two terms). [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston") and [Laredo](/wiki/Laredo,_Texas "Laredo, Texas") have similar term limits to San Antonio. All positions are elected on nonpartisan ballots, as required by Texas law. Council members are paid $45,722 and the mayor earns $61,725 a year. The current mayor is [Gina Ortiz Jones](/wiki/Gina_Ortiz_Jones "Gina Ortiz Jones"), who was elected in [2025](/wiki/2025_San_Antonio_mayoral_election "2025 San Antonio mayoral election") with 54.3% of the vote.[[180]](#cite_note-183)

The council hires a [city manager](/wiki/City_manager "City manager") to handle day-to-day operations. The council effectively functions as the city's legislative body with the city manager acting as its chief executive, responsible for the management of day-to-day operations and execution of council legislation. The current city manager is [Erik Walsh](/wiki/Erik_Walsh "Erik Walsh").

The city operates its own electric and gas utility, [CPS Energy](/wiki/CPS_Energy "CPS Energy"). The San Antonio Police Department (SAPD) is the city's municipal body of law enforcement. The San Antonio Fire Department (SAFD) provides the city with fire protection and EMS service.

The city stretches into several national congressional districts and is represented in [Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") by:[[181]](#cite_note-184)

* [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate")
  + [Ted Cruz](/wiki/Ted_Cruz "Ted Cruz") [(R)](/wiki/U.S._Republican_Party "U.S. Republican Party")
  + [John Cornyn](/wiki/John_Cornyn "John Cornyn") [(R)](/wiki/U.S._Republican_Party "U.S. Republican Party")
* [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives")
  + Texas District 20: [Joaquin Castro](/wiki/Joaquin_Castro "Joaquin Castro") [(D)](/wiki/U.S._Democratic_Party "U.S. Democratic Party")
  + Texas District 21: [Charles E. "Chip" Roy](/wiki/Chip_Roy "Chip Roy") [(R)](/wiki/U.S._Republican_Party "U.S. Republican Party")
  + Texas District 23: [Tony Gonzales](/wiki/Tony_Gonzales "Tony Gonzales") [(R)](/wiki/U.S._Republican_Party "U.S. Republican Party")
  + Texas District 28: [Henry Cuellar](/wiki/Henry_Cuellar "Henry Cuellar") [(D)](/wiki/U.S._Democratic_Party "U.S. Democratic Party")
  + Texas District 35: [Greg Casar](/wiki/Greg_Casar "Greg Casar") [(D)](/wiki/U.S._Democratic_Party "U.S. Democratic Party")
* [State Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Texas "Governor of Texas")
  + [Greg Abbott](/wiki/Greg_Abbott "Greg Abbott") [(R)](/wiki/U.S._Republican_Party "U.S. Republican Party")

### State and federal representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: State and federal representation")]

The [Texas Department of Criminal Justice](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Criminal_Justice "Texas Department of Criminal Justice") (TDCJ) operates the Parole Division Region IV headquarters in the San Antonio Metro Parole Complex. San Antonio district parole offices I and III are in the parole complex, while office II is in another location.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

The [Texas Department of Transportation](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Transportation "Texas Department of Transportation") operates the San Antonio District Office in San Antonio.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

The [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") operates San Antonio's main post office.[[184]](#cite_note-187) Other post offices are located throughout San Antonio.

### Growth policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Growth policy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Ahbroadway.jpg/250px-Ahbroadway.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ahbroadway.jpg)

Broadway Street in [Alamo Heights](/wiki/Alamo_Heights,_Texas "Alamo Heights, Texas"), an enclaved city

Unlike most large cities in the U.S., San Antonio is not completely surrounded by independent suburban cities, and under Texas state law it exercises [extraterritorial jurisdiction](/wiki/Extraterritorial_jurisdiction "Extraterritorial jurisdiction") (ETJ) over much of the surrounding [unincorporated](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") land,[[186]](#cite_note-189)[[187]](#cite_note-190) including planning major thoroughfares and enforcing rules for platting and subdivision. It pursues an aggressive annexation policy and opposes the creation of other municipalities within its ETJ.[[188]](#cite_note-191) Nearly three-fourths of its land area has been annexed since 1960.[[189]](#cite_note-192)

In the 2000s the city annexed several long narrow corridors along major thoroughfares in outlying areas to facilitate eventual annexation of growth developing along the routes. The city planned to annex nearly 40 additional square miles by 2009.[[190]](#cite_note-193)

In May 2010, the City of San Antonio agreed to release thousands of acres of land in its extraterritorial jurisdiction along Interstate 10 to [Schertz](/wiki/Schertz "Schertz"). The agreement releases a total of 3,486 acres (14.11 km2) of San Antonio's ETJ lands north of I-10 to Schertz. The ETJ lands are in an area bordered by FM 1518 to the west, Lower Seguin Road to the north, Cibolo Creek to the east and I-10 to the south.[[191]](#cite_note-194)

Involuntary annexation is a controversial issue in those parts of unincorporated Bexar County affected by it. Residents attracted to the outlying areas by lower taxes and affordable real estate values often see annexation as a mechanism to increase [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") rates (which are primarily driven by school district taxes, not city taxes) without a corresponding improvement in services such as police and fire protection, while the city regards its annexation policy as essential to its overall prosperity.[[192]](#cite_note-195)

Since the city has annexed areas over time, San Antonio surrounds several independent [enclave cities](/wiki/Inner_suburb "Inner suburb"), including [Alamo Heights](/wiki/Alamo_Heights,_Texas "Alamo Heights, Texas"), [Balcones Heights](/wiki/Balcones_Heights "Balcones Heights"), [Castle Hills](/wiki/Castle_Hills,_Texas "Castle Hills, Texas"), [Hill Country Village](/wiki/Hill_Country_Village,_Texas "Hill Country Village, Texas"), [Hollywood Park](/wiki/Hollywood_Park,_Texas "Hollywood Park, Texas"), [Kirby](/wiki/Kirby,_Texas "Kirby, Texas"), [Leon Valley](/wiki/Leon_Valley,_Texas "Leon Valley, Texas"), [Olmos Park](/wiki/Olmos_Park,_Texas "Olmos Park, Texas"), [Shavano Park](/wiki/Shavano_Park,_Texas "Shavano Park, Texas"), and [Terrell Hills](/wiki/Terrell_Hills,_Texas "Terrell Hills, Texas").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in San Antonio](/wiki/Education_in_San_Antonio "Education in San Antonio")

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Higher education")]

[![UTSA](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/UTSA_Entrance_Sign.JPG/250px-UTSA_Entrance_Sign.JPG)](/wiki/File:UTSA_Entrance_Sign.JPG)

The [University of Texas at San Antonio](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas at San Antonio")

San Antonio hosts over 100,000 students in its 31 higher-education institutions. Publicly supported schools include [UT Health San Antonio](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Health_Science_Center_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio"), the [University of Texas at San Antonio](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas at San Antonio"), [Texas A&M University–San Antonio](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University%E2%80%93San_Antonio "Texas A&M University–San Antonio"), [Troy University-San Antonio Student Support Center](/wiki/Troy_University "Troy University"), and the [Alamo Community College District](/wiki/Alamo_Community_College_District "Alamo Community College District"). The University of Texas at San Antonio is San Antonio's largest university.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Trinity_University_%28Texas%29.jpg/250px-Trinity_University_%28Texas%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trinity_University_(Texas).jpg)

[Trinity University](/wiki/Trinity_University_(Texas) "Trinity University (Texas)")

Private universities include [Trinity University](/wiki/Trinity_University_(Texas) "Trinity University (Texas)"), [St. Mary's University](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_University,_Texas "St. Mary's University, Texas"), [Our Lady of the Lake University](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_the_Lake_University "Our Lady of the Lake University"), [University of the Incarnate Word](/wiki/University_of_the_Incarnate_Word "University of the Incarnate Word"), [Webster University](/wiki/Webster_University "Webster University"), [Baptist University of the Américas](/wiki/Baptist_University_of_the_Am%C3%A9ricas "Baptist University of the Américas"), [Hallmark University](/wiki/Hallmark_University "Hallmark University"), [Oblate School of Theology](/wiki/Oblate_School_of_Theology "Oblate School of Theology"), [ECPI University](/wiki/ECPI_University "ECPI University"),[[193]](#cite_note-196) and the Southwest School of Art, which enrolled its first BFA class in 2014.[[194]](#cite_note-197)[[195]](#cite_note-198) The [San Antonio Public Library](/wiki/San_Antonio_Public_Library "San Antonio Public Library") serves all of these institutions along with the 19 independent school districts within the Bexar County and Greater San Antonio metropolitan area.[[196]](#cite_note-199) San Antonio is also home to a campus of [The Culinary Institute of America](/wiki/The_Culinary_Institute_of_America "The Culinary Institute of America").[[197]](#cite_note-200)

The [National Autonomous University of Mexico](/wiki/National_Autonomous_University_of_Mexico "National Autonomous University of Mexico") (UNAM), one of Latin America's most prestigious universities, has a campus in San Antonio.[[198]](#cite_note-201)[[199]](#cite_note-202)

The city is home to more than 30 private schools and [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools "Charter schools"). The [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Antonio](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_San_Antonio "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Antonio") operates parochial Catholic schools in San Antonio. San Antonio's private and charter schools include: [Keystone School](/wiki/Keystone_School "Keystone School"), [St. Gerard Catholic High School](/wiki/St._Gerard_Catholic_High_School "St. Gerard Catholic High School"), [Central Catholic Marianist High School](/wiki/Central_Catholic_Marianist_High_School "Central Catholic Marianist High School"), [Incarnate Word High School](/wiki/Incarnate_Word_High_School_(San_Antonio,_Texas) "Incarnate Word High School (San Antonio, Texas)"), [Saint Mary's Hall](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Hall_(San_Antonio) "St. Mary's Hall (San Antonio)"), [The Atonement Academy](/wiki/The_Atonement_Academy "The Atonement Academy"), [Antonian College Preparatory High School](/wiki/Antonian_College_Preparatory_High_School "Antonian College Preparatory High School"), [San Antonio Academy](/wiki/San_Antonio_Academy "San Antonio Academy"), [Holy Cross High School](/wiki/Holy_Cross_High_School_(San_Antonio,_Texas) "Holy Cross High School (San Antonio, Texas)"), [Providence High School](/wiki/Providence_High_School_(San_Antonio) "Providence High School (San Antonio)"), [The Carver Academy](/wiki/The_Carver_Academy "The Carver Academy"), [Keystone School](/wiki/Keystone_School "Keystone School"), [TMI – The Episcopal School of Texas](/wiki/TMI_%E2%80%93_The_Episcopal_School_of_Texas "TMI – The Episcopal School of Texas"), [St. Anthony Catholic High School](/wiki/St._Anthony_Catholic_High_School "St. Anthony Catholic High School"), [Lutheran High School of San Antonio](/wiki/Lutheran_High_School_of_San_Antonio "Lutheran High School of San Antonio"), and [Harmony Science Academy](/wiki/Harmony_Science_Academy "Harmony Science Academy") (School of Science and Technology).

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Military")]

San Antonio is home to U.S. Air Force Basic Military Training (AFBMT).[[202]](#cite_note-205) The Air Force only has one location for enlisted basic training: the 737th Training Group, at [Lackland Air Force Base](/wiki/Lackland_Air_Force_Base "Lackland Air Force Base"). All new Air Force recruits go through the same basic training at Lackland. Each year, over 35,000 new recruits go through AFBMT. In addition, METC (the Military Education and Training Campus), which provides the medical training for the U.S. military at [Fort Sam Houston](/wiki/Fort_Sam_Houston "Fort Sam Houston"), hosts 30 programs and over 24,000 annual graduates. It is the largest medical education center in the world.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in San Antonio](/wiki/Media_in_San_Antonio "Media in San Antonio")

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Print")]

San Antonio has one major newspaper, the *[San Antonio Express-News](/wiki/San_Antonio_Express-News "San Antonio Express-News")*, which has served the area since 1865. The *Express-News* circulates as the largest newspaper service in [South Texas](/wiki/South_Texas "South Texas"). The [Hearst Corporation](/wiki/Hearst_Corporation "Hearst Corporation"), which owned a second newspaper, the *San Antonio Light*, purchased the *Express-News* from [News Corp.](/wiki/News_Corporation_(1980%E2%80%932013) "News Corporation (1980–2013)") in 1992 and shut down the *Light* after failing to find a buyer.

Hearst, using the *Express-News* brand, also produces *Conexión*, a weekly magazine written by an entirely Hispanic and Latin American staff with a Latino spin on weekly events. The [San Antonio *Current*](/wiki/San_Antonio_Current "San Antonio Current") is the free "alternative" paper published weekly with local political issues, art and music news, restaurant listings and reviews, and listings of events and nightlife around town. The *San Antonio Business Journal* covers general business news. *La Prensa*, a bilingual publication, also has a long history in San Antonio. They closed their doors in June 2018.[[203]](#cite_note-206) *Edible San Antonio* is San Antonio's bimonthly food magazine, it reports on the city's food culture and it's chiefs, farmers and other figures related to food.[[204]](#cite_note-207) The *[San Antonio Observer](/wiki/San_Antonio_Observer "San Antonio Observer")* is the only weekly [African American newspaper](/wiki/African-American_newspapers "African-American newspapers") in San Antonio since 1995 and the largest in South Texas.[[205]](#cite_note-208)

The *San Antonio Report*, renamed in 2020 from the Rivard Report, is the city's only digital-only news publication.[[206]](#cite_note-209) Founded in 2011 by former Express-News editor Robert Rivard, it began as a blog but has since become a non-profit news source covering civic issues.[[207]](#cite_note-210)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Radio")]

About 50 radio stations can be heard in the San Antonio area; 30 of them are in the city proper. San Antonio is home to [iHeartMedia](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia"), the largest operator of radio stations in the U.S. Its flagship, [WOAI AM-1200](/wiki/WOAI_(AM) "WOAI (AM)"), is known for its local news operation, considered among the best in the country. It is a 50,000-watt clear channel station that reaches most of North America at night. The first radio station to broadcast in South Texas was [KTSA AM-550](/wiki/KTSA "KTSA") in 1922.

The region's [National Public Radio](/wiki/NPR "NPR") member is [Texas Public Radio](/wiki/Texas_Public_Radio "Texas Public Radio"),[[208]](#cite_note-211) a group of three stations; KSTX 89.1 FM is NPR news/talk, KPAC 88.3 is a 24-hour classical music station, and KTXI 90.1 is a mix of NPR news/talk and classical music broadcast for the West Central Texas Hill Country. KSTX also broadcasts "Riverwalk Jazz", featuring Jim Cullum Jazz Band at The Landing, a fixture on the River Walk since 1963.

KRTU 91.7 is a non-commercial radio station based at Trinity University.[[209]](#cite_note-212) Unlike most other college radio stations in the U.S., the station plays jazz 17 hours a day and college rock/indie rock at night. College alternative station KSYM, 90.1 FM, is owned by the Alamo Community College District and operated by San Antonio College students; like KRTU, it plays the Third Coast music network during the day and alternative music at night.

Most Latin American stations in the area play [regional Mexican](/wiki/Regional_styles_of_Mexican_music "Regional styles of Mexican music"), [Tejano](/wiki/Tejano_music "Tejano music") or contemporary pop. On January 12, 2006, [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision")-owned [KMYO-FM](/wiki/KMYO "KMYO") "La Kalle 95.1" changed its format from Hispanic-Rhythmic Contemporary Hits to Spanish Oldies, then named "Recuerdo 95.1". On November 10, 2006, [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision") flipped KLTO Tejano 97.7's format to [reggaeton](/wiki/Reggaeton "Reggaeton") in an attempt to reintroduce the format to San Antonio. Then 97.7 was flipped again to feature a rock format. The station no longer broadcasts anything in English. While still owned by Univision, it broadcast music by artists such as [Linkin Park](/wiki/Linkin_Park "Linkin Park"), before being sold to the [Educational Media Foundation](/wiki/Educational_Media_Foundation "Educational Media Foundation") and flipping once again to [Air1](/wiki/Air1 "Air1").

95.1 was then flipped back to the "La Kalle" format again after being flipped to feature a "95X" format. KLTO was acquired and is operated as a simulcast of [KVBH-FM](/wiki/KVBH "KVBH") Vibe 107.5. San Antonio radio is diversified, due to an influx of non-[Tejano](/wiki/Tejano "Tejano") [Latinos](/wiki/Latinos "Latinos"), mostly from the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"), who serve in the city's various military bases, as well as immigrants from Mexico. Therefore, just like in the rest of the country, radio station conglomerates have been changing formats in San Antonio to reflect shifting [demographics](/wiki/Demographics "Demographics").

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Television")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Building_for_WOAI_channel_4_in_San_Antonio%2C_Texas.jpg/250px-Building_for_WOAI_channel_4_in_San_Antonio%2C_Texas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Building_for_WOAI_channel_4_in_San_Antonio,_Texas.jpg)

WOAI-TV is San Antonio's NBC affiliate.

Despite the relatively large size of both the city proper and the metropolitan area, San Antonio has always been a medium-sized market. It presently ranks 33rd in the United States, according to [marketing research](/wiki/Marketing_research "Marketing research") firm [ACNielsen](/wiki/ACNielsen "ACNielsen").[[210]](#cite_note-213) This is mainly because the nearby suburban and rural areas are not much larger than the city itself.

San Antonio-based television stations include [KCWX](/wiki/KCWX "KCWX") channel 2 ([MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV")), [WOAI-TV](/wiki/WOAI-TV "WOAI-TV") channel 4 ([NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), with [CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW") on DT2), [KENS](/wiki/KENS "KENS") channel 5 ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")), [KLRN](/wiki/KLRN "KLRN") channel 9 ([PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS")), [KSAT-TV](/wiki/KSAT-TV "KSAT-TV") channel 12 ([ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")), [KNIC-DT](/wiki/KNIC-DT "KNIC-DT") channel 17 ([UniMás](/wiki/UniM%C3%A1s "UniMás")), [KABB](/wiki/KABB "KABB") channel 29 ([FOX](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")), [KMYS](/wiki/KMYS "KMYS") channel 35 ([Roar](/wiki/Roar_(TV_network) "Roar (TV network)")), [KWEX-DT](/wiki/KWEX-DT "KWEX-DT") channel 41 ([Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision")) and [KVDA](/wiki/KVDA "KVDA") channel 60 ([Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo")). The market is also home to three religious stations, three independent stations and one [Internet-based station](/wiki/Internet_radio "Internet radio") (210 TV[[211]](#cite_note-214)). As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit), the San Antonio market has 65% [cable TV](/wiki/Cable_TV "Cable TV") penetration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/San_Antonio_International_Airport_Ticket_Counters_September_2014.JPG/250px-San_Antonio_International_Airport_Ticket_Counters_September_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio_International_Airport_Ticket_Counters_September_2014.JPG)

Ticket counters at [San Antonio International Airport](/wiki/San_Antonio_International_Airport "San Antonio International Airport")

[San Antonio International Airport](/wiki/San_Antonio_International_Airport "San Antonio International Airport") (SAT) is located in Uptown San Antonio, about eight miles (13 km) north of Downtown. San Antonio International Airport is the [6th busiest airport based on passenger boardings](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_the_United_States "List of the busiest airports in the United States") in Texas and 42nd in the United States as of 2025.[[212]](#cite_note-215) It has two terminals and is served by 20 airlines, 15 passenger and 5 cargo ones, serving many destinations throughout the United States and Mexico. [Stinson Municipal Airport](/wiki/Stinson_Municipal_Airport "Stinson Municipal Airport") is a reliever airport located six miles (9.7 km) south of Downtown San Antonio. The airport has two runways serving primarily [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") and is also home to the [Texas Air Museum](/wiki/Texas_Air_Museum "Texas Air Museum").

### Mass transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Mass transit")]

Further information: [VIA Metropolitan Transit](/wiki/VIA_Metropolitan_Transit "VIA Metropolitan Transit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/San_Antonio_Intersection.JPG/250px-San_Antonio_Intersection.JPG)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio_Intersection.JPG)

A VIA bus, stopped at a Downtown San Antonio intersection

A bus system is provided by the city's metropolitan transit authority, [VIA Metropolitan Transit](/wiki/VIA_Metropolitan_Transit "VIA Metropolitan Transit"). VIA began operating a [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") line known as [VIA Primo](/wiki/VIA_Primo "VIA Primo")[[213]](#cite_note-216) in December 2012, which connects [Downtown San Antonio](/wiki/Downtown_San_Antonio "Downtown San Antonio") to the [South Texas Medical Center](/wiki/South_Texas_Medical_Center "South Texas Medical Center"), the main campus of the [University of Texas at San Antonio](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas at San Antonio"), and the independent enclave city of [Leon Valley](/wiki/Leon_Valley,_Texas "Leon Valley, Texas"). Additionally, VIA also offers VIAtrans [Paratransit](/wiki/Paratransit "Paratransit") Service,[[214]](#cite_note-217) a wheelchair accessible ride-share service for people with disabilities.

In August 2010, VIA Metropolitan Transit unveiled buses that are powered by diesel-electric hybrid technology.[[215]](#cite_note-viainfo.net-218) The 30 hybrid buses were put into service on VIA's express routes to serve daily commuters across the city. This set of buses follows the introduction of new vehicles powered by compressed natural gas, which were unveiled in May 2010. In the fall of 2010, VIA took delivery of three new buses that are powered by electricity from on-board batteries. These buses serve the Downtown core area, and are the first revenue vehicles VIA operates which have zero emissions.[[215]](#cite_note-viainfo.net-218)

VIA offers 90 regular bus routes and two Downtown streetcar routes.[[216]](#cite_note-219) This includes express service from Downtown to park and ride locations in the south, west, northwest, north central and northeast sides of the city, with service to major locations such as [UTSA](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_San_Antonio "University of Texas at San Antonio"), Six Flags Fiesta Texas and [SeaWorld](/wiki/SeaWorld "SeaWorld"). VIA also offers a special service to city events including [Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") games and city parades from its park and ride locations.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Rail")]

San Antonio is served by two [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") routes: the daily Chicago to San Antonio *[Texas Eagle](/wiki/Texas_Eagle "Texas Eagle")* and the thrice-weekly New Orleans to Los Angeles *[Sunset Limited](/wiki/Sunset_Limited "Sunset Limited").*[[217]](#cite_note-220) On the days that the *Sunset Limited* operates, a section of the *Texas Eagle* continues west with it, offering Chicago to Los Angeles through service. The [old Sunset Station](/wiki/San_Antonio_station_(Texas) "San Antonio station (Texas)") is now an entertainment venue owned by VIA and neighbored by the current station and the [Alamodome](/wiki/Alamodome "Alamodome").[[218]](#cite_note-221)[[219]](#cite_note-222)[[220]](#cite_note-223)

San Antonio became the largest American city without an intra-city rail system when [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), the former largest city without such a system, [procured one in 2008](/wiki/Valley_Metro_Rail "Valley Metro Rail"). A proposed passenger rail line, Lone Star Rail, would have linked San Antonio to Austin, but was cancelled in 2016 after 19 years of planning.[[221]](#cite_note-224)

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Road")]

Further information: [List of highways in San Antonio](/wiki/List_of_highways_in_San_Antonio "List of highways in San Antonio")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/US_281%2C_San_Antonio%2C_Texas.JPG/250px-US_281%2C_San_Antonio%2C_Texas.JPG)](/wiki/File:US_281,_San_Antonio,_Texas.JPG)

US 281 southbound towards Downtown San Antonio

San Antonio is served by these major freeways:

* [I-10](/wiki/Interstate_10_in_Texas "Interstate 10 in Texas"): McDermott Freeway (Northwest) runs west toward [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas"), [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). Jose Lopez Freeway (East) runs east toward [Seguin](/wiki/Seguin,_Texas "Seguin, Texas"), [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") and [Jacksonville](/wiki/Jacksonville,_Florida "Jacksonville, Florida").
* [I-35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Texas "Interstate 35 in Texas"): Pan Am Expressway (Northeast/Southwest)—runs south toward its southern terminus [Laredo](/wiki/Laredo,_Texas "Laredo, Texas") and runs north toward [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), [Dallas–Fort Worth](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"), [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City "Oklahoma City"), [Wichita](/wiki/Wichita,_Kansas "Wichita, Kansas"), [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa"), [Minneapolis-St. Paul](/wiki/Minneapolis%E2%80%93Saint_Paul "Minneapolis–Saint Paul"), and finally to its northern terminus in [Duluth](/wiki/Duluth,_Minnesota "Duluth, Minnesota").
* [I-37](/wiki/Interstate_37 "Interstate 37"): Lucian Adams Freeway (Southeast)—runs from San Antonio through its interchange with [US 281](/wiki/U.S._Route_281_in_Texas "U.S. Route 281 in Texas") south ([Edinburg](/wiki/Edinburg,_Texas "Edinburg, Texas") and [McAllen](/wiki/McAllen,_Texas "McAllen, Texas")) near [Three Rivers](/wiki/Three_Rivers,_Texas "Three Rivers, Texas") and into [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi,_Texas "Corpus Christi, Texas") through its junction with [I-69E](/wiki/Interstate_69E "Interstate 69E")/[US 77](/wiki/U.S._Route_77_in_Texas "U.S. Route 77 in Texas") south ([Kingsville](/wiki/Kingsville,_Texas "Kingsville, Texas"), [Harlingen](/wiki/Harlingen,_Texas "Harlingen, Texas") and [Brownsville](/wiki/Brownsville,_Texas "Brownsville, Texas")) to its southern terminus at Corpus Christi Bay.
* [I-410](/wiki/Interstate_410 "Interstate 410"): Connally Loop—simply called Loop 410 (four-ten) by locals is a 53-mile (85 km) inner beltway around the city.
* [US 90](/wiki/U.S._Route_90_in_Texas "U.S. Route 90 in Texas"): Cleto Rodriguez Freeway (West) through [Uvalde](/wiki/Uvalde,_Texas "Uvalde, Texas") and [Del Rio](/wiki/Del_Rio,_Texas "Del Rio, Texas") to its western terminus at I-10 in [Van Horn](/wiki/Van_Horn,_Texas "Van Horn, Texas"). Prior to I-10 East and US 90 West expressway being built US 90 traveled through the west side via West Commerce Street (westbound) and Buena Vista Street (eastbound) and Historic Old Highway 90 (known as Enrique M. Barrera Parkway from 2015 to 2022[[222]](#cite_note-oldhwy90-225)). On the east side it traveled along East Commerce Street to its current alignment which runs concurrent with I-10 East to [Seguin](/wiki/Seguin,_Texas "Seguin, Texas").
* [US 281](/wiki/U.S._Route_281_in_Texas "U.S. Route 281 in Texas"): McAllister Freeway (North) to [Johnson City](/wiki/Johnson_City,_Texas "Johnson City, Texas") and [Wichita Falls](/wiki/Wichita_Falls,_Texas "Wichita Falls, Texas"). Southbound, it runs [concurrent](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with I-37, then I-410 for 4 miles (6 km), then heads south to [Pleasanton](/wiki/Pleasanton,_Texas "Pleasanton, Texas"). Prior to I-37 and the McAllister Freeway. being built US 281 traveled through the north side via San Pedro Avenue and the south side via Roosevelt Avenue.
* [SH 151](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_151 "Texas State Highway 151"): Stotzer Freeway runs from US 90 west through Westover Hills which includes [SeaWorld](/wiki/SeaWorld_San_Antonio "SeaWorld San Antonio") to its western terminus at State Loop 1604.
* [Loop 1604](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_1604 "Texas State Highway Loop 1604"): Charles W. Anderson Loop—simply called 1604 (sixteen-oh-four) by locals—is a 96-mile (154 km) outer beltway around San Antonio.

Other highways include:

* [US 87](/wiki/U.S._Route_87_in_Texas "U.S. Route 87 in Texas"): Southbound to [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Texas "Victoria, Texas") along Roland Avenue then Rigsby Avenue. It runs concurrent with I-10 for 52 miles (84 km) where it goes to [San Angelo](/wiki/San_Angelo,_Texas "San Angelo, Texas") northbound.
* [US 181](/wiki/U.S._Route_181 "U.S. Route 181"): Starts 0.5 miles (0.8 km) south of I-410/I-37/US 281 interchange and heads toward [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi,_Texas "Corpus Christi, Texas") via [Beeville](/wiki/Beeville,_Texas "Beeville, Texas"). Prior to I-37 being built, US 181 traveled along Presa Street from Downtown to its current alignment.
* [SH 16](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_16 "Texas State Highway 16"): From [Zapata](/wiki/Zapata,_Texas "Zapata, Texas"), it runs concurrent with I-410 for 17 miles (27 km) along southwest San Antonio, over to Bandera Road to [Bandera](/wiki/Bandera,_Texas "Bandera, Texas")
* [SH 130](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_130 "Texas State Highway 130"): Starting at its southern terminus at I-35 South, it travels along East South Loop 410 until I-10/US-90, where it runs concurrently with the interstate until outside of [Seguin](/wiki/Seguin,_Texas "Seguin, Texas"), turning into a state toll road.
* [SH Spur 421](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Spur_421 "Texas State Highway Spur 421"): Also known more commonly as "Culebra Road" and "Bandera Road" inside Loop 410.
* [SH Spur 422](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Spur_422 "Texas State Highway Spur 422"): Known as the Poteet Jourdanton Freeway. It was originally planned to have a high speed direct connection to I-35.
* [Loop 345](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_345 "Texas State Highway Loop 345"): Fredericksburg Road by locals; is the business loop for I-10 West/US-87 North.[[223]](#cite_note-226)
* [Loop 368](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_368 "Texas State Highway Loop 368"): Broadway and Austin Highway by locals; is the business loop for I-35 North.[[224]](#cite_note-227)
* [State PA 1502 (Wurzbach Parkway)](/wiki/Wurzbach_Parkway "Wurzbach Parkway"): Limited-access, high speed road parallel to north IH-410. It has connections to I-35 (via O'Connor Road) and I-10 (via Wurzbach Road) across the north side of town.
* [Loop 353](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_353 "Texas State Highway Loop 353"): Nogalitos Street and New Laredo Highway is the business loop for I-35 South.[[225]](#cite_note-228)
* [Loop 13](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_13 "Texas State Highway Loop 13"): Is the city's inner loop on the south side serving [Lackland AFB](/wiki/Lackland_Air_Force_Base "Lackland Air Force Base"), [Port San Antonio](/wiki/Port_San_Antonio "Port San Antonio"), South Park Mall and [Brooks CityBase](/wiki/Brooks_City-Base "Brooks City-Base") traveling along Military Drive on the south side and WW White Road on the east side to its junction with I-35/I-410. The northern arc of the loop is now I-410.

Along with [FM 471](/wiki/Farm_to_Market_Road_471 "Farm to Market Road 471"), [FM 1957](/wiki/Farm_to_Market_Road_1957 "Farm to Market Road 1957"), and [SH 211](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_211 "Texas State Highway 211")

Also, the city has multiple streets with the same (or similar) names. As examples:

* "Military Drive", "Military Highway.", and "Military Drive West"—Military Drive loops around the western and southern parts of the city. Military Highway, also called "NW Military Drive" by the locals, serves the northwestern part of the city. Military Drive West serves the far western portion of the city. None intersect each other.
* "Wurzbach Road", "Wurzbach Parkway", and "Harry Wurzbach Road"—Wurzbach Road serves the northwestern part of the city. Wurzbach Parkway, an expressway, is an east–west road serving the northwestern and northeastern parts of the city (and can be considered an extension of Wurzbach Road). Harry Wurzbach Road runs past Fort Sam Houston and Terrell Hills on the city's northeast side, and it does not intersect Wurzbach Road or Wurzbach Parkway.
* "Hausman Road" and "South Hausman Road" are two roads serving [Helotes](/wiki/Helotes,_Texas "Helotes, Texas") and the far northwest side of the city. Hausman is a major road, and a route used by locals of Helotes and NW San Antonio as an alternative to Loop 1604 connecting to I-10. South Hausman is a suburban road that does not connect with Hausman (despite its name).

A large portion of trade between Mexico and the United States passes through the San Antonio area's interstate highway system.[[226]](#cite_note-229)

Of the five largest cities in Texas, San Antonio (within city limits) is the largest city in the state without toll roads. (Houston, Dallas, Austin, and Fort Worth have toll roads.)

San Antonio enjoys less traffic congestion than other large Texas cities. In a 2022 study by TomTom, San Antonio is only the 41st-most congested city in the U.S. and the fifth-most congested city in Texas. By comparison, Houston, McAllen, Austin, and Dallas-Fort Worth rank higher than San Antonio for traffic congestion.[[227]](#cite_note-230)

### Bicycle paths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Bicycle paths")]

San Antonio has about 136 miles (219 km) of bike lanes, routes or off-road paths.[[228]](#cite_note-231) Off-road trails travel along the [San Antonio River](/wiki/San_Antonio_River "San Antonio River"), linear greenways, or city parks. Although largely disconnected, the progress to create a bicycle-friendly environment was recognized when San Antonio was designated a bronze-level "Bicycle Friendly Community" in 2015 by the League of American Bicyclists.[[229]](#cite_note-232)

### Bicycle sharing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Bicycle sharing")]

A bike sharing service was approved by the city council on June 17, 2010.[[230]](#cite_note-233) The initial program consisted of 140 bikes at 14 locations supported by a "central hub". It is expected to serve both residents and visitors. San Antonio Bike Share, a non-profit, was formed to oversee the operation, locally operated and maintained by Bike World. B-Cycle, the same system used in Denver, supplies the bike share system. It began operation in March 2011.[[231]](#cite_note-234)

### Walkability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Walkability")]

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio&action=edit), [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score") ranks San Antonio as the least [walkable](/wiki/Walkability "Walkability") American city a population greater than one million and calls it car-dependent. This is due in large part to its rapid growth after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the prevalence of [single-family zoning](/wiki/Single-family_zoning "Single-family zoning") and thousands of miles of missing or broken [sidewalks](/wiki/Sidewalk "Sidewalk").[[232]](#cite_note-235)

The [Howard W. Peak](/wiki/Howard_W._Peak "Howard W. Peak") Greenway is an 84-mile system (135 km) of hiking and biking trails that roughly forms a ring around the city. It is accessible by more than 65 trailheads and connects to dozens of parks. With construction beginning in 2007, the trails consist of the [Leon Creek](/wiki/Leon_Creek "Leon Creek") Greenway, the [Salado Creek](/wiki/Salado_Creek "Salado Creek") Greenway, the Westside Creeks, and the [Medina River](/wiki/Medina_River "Medina River") Greenway.[[233]](#cite_note-236)[[234]](#cite_note-237)
